resources.string: Lesson 8: Reading Basics
Learn how to read Japanese sentences and words.
Introduction

Reading Japanese is a rewarding journey that combines three different scripts. Once you know the characters, the next step is understanding how they flow together to form words and sentences. This lesson will guide you through the fundamental rules of Japanese reading.
Reading Direction

In modern Japanese, text is often written horizontally from left to right, just like English. however, you will also see traditional vertical writing, which is read from top to bottom, starting from the rightmost column.
Basic Words
Here are some common words written in Hiragana and Katakana. Try reading them aloud!
Double Consonants (Sokuon)

The small "っ" (tsu) or "ッ" indicates a double consonant. It acts as a brief pause in the breath before the next sound. 

Example: 
がっこう (Gakkou) - School 
コップ (Koppu) - Cup
Long Vowels

Vowels can be extended to change a word's meaning. In Hiragana, we add another vowel (usually "u" or "i"). In Katakana, we use a long dash "ー". 

Example: 
おとうさん (Otousan) - Father 
ケーキ (Keeki) - Cake
Contracted Sounds (Yōon)

Small versions of ya, yu, and yo (ゃ, ゅ, ょ) combine with certain characters to create a single syllable. 

Example: 
チャンス (Chansu) - Chance 
きょう (Kyou) - Today
Essential Particles
Particles are small markers that show the relationship between words in a sentence. Note that some change their pronunciation when used as particles.
は (Wa) - Topic Marker
Though written as "ha", it is pronounced "wa" when marking the topic. \nExample: ねこ は かわいい (Neko wa kawaii) - Cats are cute.
へ (E) - Direction Marker
Though written as "he", it is pronounced "e" when indicating direction. \nExample: とうきょう へ いく (Toukyou e iku) - Go to Tokyo.
を (Wo/O) - Object Marker
This character is exclusively used as a particle to mark the direct object of a verb. \nExample: みず を のむ (Mizu o nomu) - Drink water.
